prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 About me: I'm a 31-year-old Black man.
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I had a cold last week (sore throat and congestion) but I still feel more tired than usual - my energy hasn't fully bounced back. No current
 fever.
 - History: No autoimmune disease and not on any regular medications.
 - Vitals: BP 112/68, HR 70, Temp 36.7°C
 - Exam: Normal exam at my visit.
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 3.3 K/uL (4.0-11.0) low
 - Absolute neutrophil count (ANC): 1.5 K/uL (1.5-7.0) borderline
 - Hemoglobin: 13.1 g/dL (12.0-16.0) normal
 - Platelets: 220 K/uL (150-400) normal
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D – choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24–48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's
 here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your white blood cell count (WBC) is slightly below the normal reference range, and your absolute neutrophil count (ANC) is right at the lower border of normal. This is worth noting but has important context. First, many Black individuals naturally have lower baseline WBC and neutrophil counts — a well-recognized finding called benign ethnic neutropenia — which is not a disease and doesn't increase infection risk. Second, viral infections like the cold you just had commonly cause a temporary dip in white blood cells that resolves on its own over days to weeks. Your lingering fatigue is also very typical of post-viral recovery. Your hemoglobin, platelets, vitals, and physical exam are all normal, which is reassuring. The most likely explanation is a combination of your normal baseline and a transient post-viral effect. A repeat blood count in a few weeks would confirm everything is trending back to your normal.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your white blood cell count (WBC) is mildly low at 3.3, and your absolute neutrophil count (ANC) is right at the lower border of the normal range at 1.5. The most likely explanation is that your body is still recovering from the viral cold you had last week. Viral infections commonly cause a temporary dip in white blood cells, and this often resolves on its own within a few weeks. Importantly, your hemoglobin and platelets are completely normal, your vitals are reassuring, and your exam was normal — all good signs that this is not something more serious right now. It's also worth noting that benign ethnic neutropenia is relatively common in Black individuals, meaning a mildly lower WBC/ANC can be a normal baseline for you. A follow-up recheck in a few weeks would help confirm your counts are recovering.

TRIAGE: B

CONFIDENCE: 88%

The best next step is to have these labs rechecked in a few weeks once you've fully recovered from your cold, to see if they normalize.

Second, benign ethnic neutropenia is a well-recognized, harmless condition in people of African descent where baseline neutrophil counts naturally run lower — sometimes right around 1.5 or even a bit below — without any increased infection risk. A repeat blood count in a few weeks would help confirm everything is trending back to normal.
